data.title: Sony Group: A Wide Moat Compounder At A 15x P/E Gift (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
It's been a few months since my Sony (SONY) article. My first coverage was right after fiscal Q2 2026, and in short, my opinion of the stock was that, although it is a potential compounder with wide moats, it was not the time to buy. There were still some uncertainties regarding the gaming market, but mainly, the valuation was not good enough.

Since then, SONY stock has declined 30%. So, now, the scenario seems completely different to me. The Valuation? Much better. There is already a margin of safety and asymmetry. But besides that, there are also important triggers for Sony in the coming quarters that can unlock a lot of value, and I think the market is not pricing this correctly, which makes the case even more appealing.

For this reason, I am upgrading my rating on Sony to a buy.

Sony: A Word About Recent Results and Moats

Recapping the case a bit, the reason I like Sony a lot for the long term is its diversification into businesses with wide moats. Here, we truly have that meaning of a large Japanese conglomerate that was very famous a few decades ago. There is even a term for this, which I don't think is very famous today: Keiretsu, which focuses on various shareholdings simultaneously with a collaborative long-term focus.

Sony is not a video game company. I also don't think it can be said to be exactly an entertainment company, although the majority of revenue today is related to the video game, movies, or music industry. Because besides these three, Sony is still a global leader in camera sensors (used in smartphones) and so on.

The table below shows well how the revenue is diversified. We have strong revenue in games & network services, which comes from streams like hardware sales and services related to the PlayStation 5. But it is also possible to notice how Imaging & Sensing (the sensors mentioned earlier) is quite strong and has a high operating margin, which makes it still relevant in operating income (about 22%).

The Music division is also very interesting. Music royalties are a very good business. The reason is mainly the characteristic of resilience. Royalties from famous songs recorded 10, or 20 years ago continue to generate revenue to this day; it is a very rich asset. Movies released in 2026, in fact, movies that will still be released in 2030, could very well use soundtracks from artists of previous decades. Likewise, people will continue to use music streaming (read: Spotify (SPOT), YouTube Music, and similar) to listen to songs that Sony Group owns for years and years, generating more revenue at a low cost, almost as if it were an asset that generates "passive" income in an almost perpetual way.

Proving this point, the top-line CAGR was almost 15% in the last 4 years, and the operating income CAGR was 18% in that same period.

Even though it is a resilient business, it is not a boring business. There is a lot happening in the industry; recently Sony Music bought the Queen catalog, for instance. This means that with every play someone gives to Bohemian Rhapsody on Spotify, a small revenue is generated for Sony.

Returning to the rest of the operations, even if Music revenue is smaller than Games, Entertainment and Imaging, its operating income is larger than all of these, being responsible for almost a third of the operating income from continued operations.

And this was something I mentioned in my last article. Even though it is very diverse, the businesses end up complementing each other. A cinema IP can be used in games, and then there are the intangible benefits like increasing the notoriety of an IP in cinema to generate demand for games, but also tangible benefits like decreasing costs with music royalties, and verticalization of the operation (producing the hardware, which is the PS5 console, but also producing the services, producing exclusive games, etc.).

Moving to the most recent results, fiscal Q3 was not very good news; revenue remained flat YoY, but I liked that operating income increased 22%, driven not only by an optimization of the "corporate and elimination" part but also by the better margin of the Imaging & Sensing segment. If you go back to the first image of the article, you will also see that in Feb., there were positive revisions for the FY, with better sales expectations driven by games, and also better operating income driven by music and Imaging & Sensing solutions.

In short, what I want to say here is that Sony continues to be an outstanding company. Its business is "spot-on," with solid moats, and solid prospects for games, music, and similar, and all of this makes it possible to envision a compounding in the long term.

The GTA VI Trigger

Besides all these positive aspects in the long term, there is a very interesting trigger for the end of the calendar year 2026: GTA VI.

I did a very complete analysis of Take-Two Interactive (TTWO), and if you want to understand the trigger from Take-Two's point of view in more detail, I suggest you read it here. But summarizing the trigger, GTA VI is probably the most anticipated product in the entertainment industry ever. Expectations are for sales of millions of units in the first days of release, and dozens of millions of units throughout the first year.

This in itself already benefits Sony. With an installed base of ~90 million PS5s around the world, if it sells ~30 million units of a third-party game, it means literally hundreds of millions of dollars just from the platform fee (take rate).

But I think the benefit for Sony goes beyond software sales. I believe there will be a strong push for PS5 and PS5 Pro sales because of GTA VI. Many people who do not own a console will want to play. And this comes at a moment when components are more expensive (like SSDs and memory), and Sony just did a price hike on PS5s (check it in this news). I don't think a $100 price increase will decrease demand, due to the GTA VI trigger.

Now the PS5 Pro, which is a version that should handle GTA VI in a more "optimized" way, is costing $899 in the US, while the PS5 digital edition is costing $599, and the PS5 "disk edition" is at $649. Let's assume that GTA VI is a catalyst for selling 1 million PS5 Pros and 2.5 million PS5 digital editions. That would be $2.4 billion in revenue. Estimated revenue for Sony's FY is ~$28 billion (¥4.6 trillion). That is, for the next fiscal year, we are talking about a growth of more than 8% just with console sales, and that is in reasonable estimates. The current scenario could very well trigger sales of 5 million PS5s in total, not to mention if there is any special edition, or some combo with the PS6 in the coming ones, which would generate even more value.

Another point is that this increases the installed base, so if the player bought the PS5 to play GTA VI, they will probably subscribe to PlayStation products, and buy another PlayStation game during the year, and this increases revenue structurally.

Risks, Valuation and Bottom Line

This does not mean that there are no risks for Sony. Even with these tailwinds, we are still going through a complex scenario that ends up creating some headwinds. Production cost is one of them. I imagine that console sales in 2026 will be good enough to create an operating leverage. But if costs continue rising, the hardware margin continues to be pressured, even with these trends. This could reach a level where either Sony absorbs the costs significantly or increases the price (even more), which could generate a negative reaction. Besides that, we also have execution risk, such as game production (which costs a lot) that may not give the desired result, like some live services that were failures, such as Concord.

Furthermore, as it is a verticalized company and operates in console hardware but also in Imaging & Sensing, there are also impacts from geopolitical trends beyond the supply chain, such as tariffs, which affect demand for its products and the margin. Besides that, being a global company, FX also affects a lot. So if there is a massive devaluation in the JPY, this affects the consolidated revenue (when we look in USD).

But even if these risks have become a bit more evident in the last quarters, I think SONY stock has become more attractive. Besides the triggers and the solidity, the valuation has become much better. The drop of almost 30% in the stock in recent months has brought the forward P/E to 15x.

15x earnings for a compounder with clear triggers, which on average grew EBIT by ~12% in the last 10 years, is very cheap. For comparison purposes, the avg. of the last 5y of Sony's fwd P/E was 18x, i.e., there is a 13% discount relative to this average.

That's why I think that now, a buy rating is worth it for Sony stock.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Service Properties Trust Is Navigating Their Debt Problem
data.description:
History Doesn't Repeat Itself, But It Often Rhymes

-Mark Twain

There has been trouble in the markets over the past several months. Everywhere you look, there appear to be new problems popping up. First, it was the private credit world that began to show cracks. Then came problems in the software sector and revaluation of technology. Now, the world is facing an energy crisis as the US charges into a war of choice in the Middle East.

For the real estate sector, this volatility could not come at a worse time. The past five years have been difficult for real estate companies of all shapes and sizes. Shifting interest rates and supply and demand imbalances have caused problems across commercial real estate. The tide has certainly been going out, and the past several years have shown us who has been swimming naked.

Today, we revisit Service Properties Trust (SVC). This article will focus on recent capital markets activity and follow up on my prior coverage of the externally managed REIT.

Review of Prior Coverage

I have covered SVC on several occasions over the past few years. My most recent coverage was published on Seeking Alpha around a year ago. In the article, I reviewed SVC, including an overview of the company’s portfolio of commercial assets and balance sheet. The focus of the article was on the risks in the company’s capital structure.

Despite high-quality assets, SVC faces significant financial challenges, including negative CAD, rising interest expenses, and a daunting debt wall.

I rated the company a Sell given the risk factors, despite a relatively creditworthy portfolio and prudent steps to wind down their capex-heavy hotel vertical. Since publication, SVC shares have been volatile.

My thought process was that SVC’s large upcoming debt maturities were likely to create significant financial trouble for the REIT.

Over the next three years, SVC will refinance around $2 billion in debt into a higher interest rate environment. The interest rates on this debt range from 4.75% to 5.25%, within 100 basis points of the current risk free rate.

This debt maturing inside of three years could pose a material financing risk for a company whose equity now makes up less than 10% of enterprise value. With the equity value continuing to deteriorate, SVC’s leverage ratios are beginning to look rather scary. Over the past twelve months, the company’s interest coverage ratio has declined to 1.5x and net debt to EBITDAre has reached nearly 10x.

At the time, SVC appeared to be at a fork in the road. After years of running a high-leverage, growth-oriented business, the company was forced to reassess and make a strategic pivot. Recapitalization appeared to be the only solution for the math problem, and it became a race to free up capital quickly enough to pay down upcoming debt.

What Just Happened?

SVC experienced one of the largest single-day drops in the company’s history. Massive price movements are uncommon for REITs of any kind given the steady eddy nature of real estate. So what exactly is going on with SVC?

The answer lies in recent capital markets activity.

SVC just announced a $500 million equity offering.

Service Properties Trust (SVC) today announced that it has commenced a $500.0 million underwritten public offering of its common shares of beneficial interest. SVC expects to use the net proceeds of this proposed offering to redeem all or a portion of the $100.0 million principal amount outstanding of its 4.95% senior notes due 2027 and/or the $450.0 million principal amount outstanding of its 5.50% senior notes due 2027.

The question then shifts to why a company whose share price has declined so significantly is issuing millions of shares?

In my prior article, I highlighted that the company has a significant amount of debt that will be refinanced from 2026 to 2028. When we discuss debt maturities and debt refinancing, we often take an assumptive approach.

We look at interest rate spreads, including changes in credit rating and market interest rates, which may affect the coupon rate of newly issued debt. This approach overlooks the assumption that the company will be able to issue that debt.

This is not always the case.

History Rhymes

If we are to rewind the clock nearly 20 years, we can have a clear view of what led to the 2008 financial crisis. Amidst the chaos, REITs were some of the most impacted companies. Many saw their stock prices impacted for ten years, including some of the best and brightest, like Prologis (PLD).

We know the value of real estate was impacted by the economic downturn, but why did these companies perform so poorly, and why did their share prices struggle to recover?

The answer lies not in asset-level problems but in capital markets activity. Prior to the financial crisis, many REITs opted for a short debt ladder. This front-loaded maturities and positioned the company to constantly roll over its debt, presumably into lower rates.

This made sense considering long-term rates had been on a multidecade decline.

For REITs, the benefit was tangible, and earnings began to reap the benefits. However, an unseen risk emerged to these companies. These companies were forced to answer a question that nobody thought to ask. What if you can't refinance your debt?

During the financial crisis, highly levered REITs were forced to issue massive numbers of shares at impaired share prices in order to meet their debt obligations. As illustrated below, there is a correlation between the downturn of PLD shares during the Great Recession and their share issuances.

Chris Volk talks about what he calls cash flow immunization. The general idea is that if your debt maturities in any given year are lower than free cash flow, the possibility of bankruptcy is nearly nil. This is simply because you have enough cash to pay down the debt without having to turn to an alternative capital source.

When you don’t have enough cash to meet your near-term obligations, you carry a real financing risk. From the simplest perspective, not having sufficient cash is the reason companies go bankrupt.

So how does all of this relate to SVC?

Turning Back

In my prior article, we talked about the risks behind SVC’s upcoming debt maturities. The company was selling assets to free up cash that could pay down maturing debt; however, it was a race against the clock. With around $2 billion maturing over a three-year period, time was running out for SVC.

SVC’s decision to issue $500 million of equity comes at a time when the company’s share price is at all-time lows. My belief is that the company was unable to secure new debt to refinance their maturing 2027 senior notes, given the wider systemic issues facing credit. A massive share issuance this late in the game is usually not a good sign. For reference, $500 million of shares is more than double the company’s current equity market cap.

Another parallel to draw is what has been happening over the past year at Hudson Pacific Properties (HPP). Although HPP has an entirely different business with different assets in different geographies, the companies are facing similar financial challenges. Maturing debt created a math problem that HPP was forced to solve.

The company held off on pulling the equity lever for several years in hopes that the market could turn around. In hindsight, this was the wrong move, and HPP was forced to issue shares at a late stage in their business cycle.

Issuing such a large number of shares creates a math problem. With 166 million shares outstanding as of year-end, the issuance will approximately triple the outstanding share count. Looking forward to SVC, issuing hundreds of millions of new shares more than doubles the existing share count. This makes the possibility of cash flow per share making a rebound much more difficult since cash available for distribution will now be further divided by more shares. This means the dividend is even less likely to make a material rebound soon.

Now it appears as though the company is facing the music, and issuing hundreds of millions of shares is the best option to pay down upcoming debt.

Conclusion

Over the past several years, investors have pointed to creditworthy assets as being the foundation of SVC. After all, the company’s top tenant in the net lease sleeve is TravelCenters of America, accounting for 44% of net lease rent. The tenant is creditworthy, and the chances of bankruptcy are low. However, with any REIT, you need to look past the assets and see the trouble that may be lurking in the weeds.

After all, a REIT’s share price reflects the current financial performance of the operations, not the value of the underlying assets. Share price reflects the market’s perception of forward risk and other factors. In this case, shares are also affected by the math behind capital markets activity. SVC earns a continued Sell rating.

data.title: adidas: Valuation Is Not Reflecting The Improved Fundamentals (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Summary

I gave a hold rating to adidas (ADDYY) back in Dec. '24. I was worried that ADDYY may continue to miss guidance despite the positive indicators seen. I am upgrading to a buy today, as the business has now recovered enough for me to turn bullish. Growth is broader, North America is no longer a drag, and the product quality/breadth looks much healthier than before. While 2026 EBIT may look weak because of tariff and FX headwinds, I think those issues are external. The more important point is that the underlying demand and brand momentum still look strong.

Business has recovered

After monitoring ADDYY for more than a year, I believe I have seen enough evidence that this business has recovered. For perspective, in FY2025, ADDYY reported EUR24.8 billion in revenue, with the adidas brand revenue growing 13% y/y on an FX-neutral (FXN) basis; gross margin also expanded to 51.6%, and EBIT grew significantly, by 54% y/y, to ~EUR2.06 billion. This gets even better when you consider that 2025 had no Yeezy revenue contribution, while 2024 had ~EUR650 million of Yeezy sales and around EUR200 million of operating profit from Yeezy. Even if we look at the Q4 numbers, adidas brand revenue grew 11% y/y FXN, and EBIT more than doubled to EUR164 million. Aside from the headline numbers, the quality of this growth has turned a lot stronger too.

Growth was evident across all key segments: footwear grew 12%, apparel grew 15%, and performance grew 15%. The momentum exited the year in a strong manner wherein, in Q4, apparel grew 20%, performance grew 27%, and running within footwear grew >35%. As for comfort and lifestyle running, I would think this is going to start contributing to growth as well. It was nice to hear management acknowledging that other brands had recently been more dominant there and that ADDYY did not have the right silhouettes. This is a good start because it shows that ADDYY is aware of the opportunity and where the problem is, which is why I think Hyperboost matters. Per management description, this is an extension of performance into lifestyle (one example is the new Boost foam, which was noted as 40% lighter than old Boost). Putting aside whether that is true or not, more importantly, this is a good example of ADDYY being capable of extending a segment to ride on an existing consumer trend (specifically, I mean running, which saw growth accelerate in 2025). This Hyperboost effectively gives ADDYY another way to carry that momentum into comfort and lifestyle.

Finally, we have the North America performance that has recovered really well. The adidas brand grew 10% y/y FXN in North America, and Q4 still grew 5% even with management taking a conservative wholesale sell-in approach. As a recap, the North American region has been the drag on the business, and if it had stayed weak, it would be hard to argue that the turnaround was complete. Instead, it is now back to growth, and that should give investors comfort that the worst is over.

Growth guide and Q1 2026 earnings preview

The main reason why the market has reacted, I believe, is because management guided 2026 EBIT to ~EUR2.3 billion, and they also said higher US tariffs and unfavorable FX are expected to weigh on EBIT by ~EUR400 million. On face value, the near-term earnings bridge looks non-eventful, but I think that misses the more important point. The headwinds cited are external factors and not a sign that demand is rolling over or that the recovery trend is losing strength. The right guide to look at is the FXN growth guide, for which management still expects a high-single-digit rate in 2026. Note that this is against a 12.4% y/y FXN growth in FY25, which is on top of 12% in FY24. On a three-year stack basis, FY26 is going to print >30% FXN growth, which is the strongest ever since Covid.

As for my preview for Q1 2026, I think the focus is going to be whether this growth momentum can keep going. The first thing I would watch is product breadth. Terrace staying healthy is good, but growth should still come from several places at once, especially apparel, performance, and running. If that stays intact, then the story remains much better than the old ADDYY that leaned more heavily on one lifestyle franchise.

The second thing I would focus on is North America. That region has already moved from being a drag to being a contributor. So, the question now is whether it can keep growing despite the conservative wholesale stance. The full impact of the latter is still uncertain. For all we know, consumer traffic is not flowing into ADDYY’s own stores as intended. If that happens and North America slows again, I think the recovery story in this region would come under heavy scrutiny, which in turn weighs on the stock valuation.

Lastly, I would pay close attention to margins and the profit bridge. As much as I think the headwinds in FY2026 are external, if they get worse and hurt earnings by more than expected, it could still weigh on the headline numbers. Also, I would be careful to distinguish whether any form of earnings weakness is really due to these external factors and not due to demand, pricing, or promotional intensity getting worse.

Valuation

I think the discount to Nike no longer makes as much sense as it did before. Back then, ADDYY was still trying to prove that the recovery was real. Revenue growth had been weak, execution was poor, and North America remained a drag. A discount was fair previously, but I don’t think that is the case today. What changed is that the recovery is now real, and it looks broader too. ADDYY is no longer relying on one hot franchise to grow. The business has returned to growth, North America is no longer dragging, and the growth quality looks healthier across more categories.

Even if we compare it to other brands, ADDYY's growth outlook is not extremely worse such that it deserves to trade at such a big discount. I believe ADDYY should at least trade in line with peers, at 18x forward P/E, if it can deliver the growth as expected.

Investment Risk

As I said, the risk is whatever growth momentum we see today fades away. If newer court styles, comfort products, and lifestyle running do not scale fast enough, growth could decelerate again. Tariffs and FX headwinds may be worse than what management guided, and that could weigh on reported numbers, which may limit how quickly the stock rerates.

Conclusion

I have turned bullish on ADDYY because the debate has changed. This is no longer a story about whether the recovery is real. I think that part has been proven. The focus now is whether ADDYY can continue this growth momentum. There are still risks to consider, but the setup looks much better than before with improved growth quality, and North America has recovered. Valuation is also cheap on a relative basis, making the upside more attractive.
